title: Fish and seafood production
subtitle: Fish and seafood production is measured as the sum of seafood from wild catch and fish farming (aquaculture).
caption:
Fish and seafood production, 2023

Fish and seafood production is measured as the sum of seafood from wild catch and fish farming (aquaculture).

This choropleth map displays global fish and seafood production volumes for 2023, measured in tonnes, using a color scale that ranges from pale yellow for the lowest outputs to deep navy blue for the highest. The visualization reveals a highly concentrated distribution of production, with the vast majority of the world's output originating from East Asia. China stands out as the singular global leader, shaded in the darkest blue band representing production exceeding 30 million tonnes, a volume that visually dwarfs all other nations. Following China, the map identifies a secondary tier of major producers shaded in medium-to-dark blue, including India, Indonesia, Russia, the United States, and Norway, each falling within the 3 million to 10 million tonne range. In South America, Peru and Chile also appear in these higher production bands. Across Europe, production is visible in the United Kingdom, Spain, and France, though at lower volumes than the Asian giants. Large portions of the African continent, particularly in the central and northern regions, along with countries in Central Asia like Kazakhstan and Mongolia, are shaded in pale yellow or light green, indicating production levels below 300,000 tonnes or between 300,000 and 1 million tonnes respectively. Several landlocked nations in Africa and the Middle East are marked with diagonal hatching to denote that no data is available. The geographic pattern shows that high-volume production is not evenly distributed globally but is instead clustered in specific coastal nations with significant aquaculture industries or large-scale wild capture fisheries, while vast inland regions contribute minimally to the total global tonnage.
